old_claim.note: Pottery provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct Pottery to Labor Specialization edge remains because pottery is an example of specialized craft work, not a prerequisite that enabled the broader division of labor.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica's Neolithic overview says intensive food production allowed some farming-community members to pursue specialized crafts and lists pottery as one of the crafts appearing in the period.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source presents intensive food production and farming communities as the enabler of specialized crafts, while pottery is one craft example.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because pottery is presented as a craft that specialization can produce, the source does not support pottery as an upstream prerequisite for labor specialization itself.

ontology_before: Labor Specialization depended directly on Pottery, making a particular craft example appear to be a causal prerequisite for the broader social division of work.
ontology_after: Labor Specialization depends on Agriculture as the food-production and surplus context; pottery remains a downstream or parallel craft example rather than an upstream dependency.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed the direct pottery prerequisite. Preserved: agriculture remains the source-backed enabling dependency for labor specialization.
would_reject_if: The Labor Specialization node is narrowed to pottery-specialist workshops rather than broad division of labor.
A source shows early labor specialization could only emerge after pottery technology existed.
A separate pottery_specialist_workshops node is introduced and intentionally depends on pottery.
short_reason: Pottery is an example of specialized work, not a prerequisite for labor specialization.
